seoTitle: How to Handle Errors in Power Automate Flows
metaDescription: Learn error handling in Power Automate using Configure Run After, Scopes, Try-Catch patterns, and automated failure notifications.
intro: A flow that breaks silently when something goes wrong isn't reliable enough for real business use. This guide covers how to handle errors in Power Automate using Configure Run After, Scopes, and Try-Catch style patterns, plus how to send notifications and logs when something fails.

steps.heading: Why error handling matters
steps.body: Without error handling, a single failed step can stop a whole flow or leave a process half-finished, which becomes a bigger issue as flows are used for critical tasks.

steps.heading: Configure Run After and Scopes
steps.body: Use Configure Run After settings on an action to control what happens if a prior step fails, and group related actions into Scopes to manage them together.

steps.heading: Build Try-Catch style logic
steps.body: Structure a flow with a 'try' scope containing the main actions and a 'catch' scope that runs only if something in the try scope fails, mimicking traditional error handling.

steps.heading: Send error notifications and troubleshoot
steps.body: Add actions that alert you by email or Teams when a flow fails, and use the run history to review what went wrong so you can fix the underlying issue.

faq.question: What does Configure Run After actually do?
faq.answer: It lets you specify whether an action should run based on the success, failure, skip, or timeout of a previous step, giving you control over the flow's path after an error.

faq.question: How do I build something like a Try-Catch block in Power Automate?
faq.answer: Group your main actions in a Scope, then add a second Scope configured to run only if the first one fails, which mirrors the try-catch pattern from traditional programming.

faq.question: Can Power Automate notify me automatically when a flow fails?
faq.answer: Yes, you can add an email or Teams notification action set to trigger only when the flow or a specific scope has failed.

faq.question: Should every flow have error handling?
faq.answer: It's worth adding for any flow that supports a real business process, since untested or unhandled failures can leave work incomplete without anyone noticing.
